topic: Zero Fitted Parameters — Post-EXP086 Full Rewrite Review
our_argument_summary: Section 6.1 argues that the dome's 'zero fitted parameters' claim is false. It now examines WIN-053 (both single-pole and two-pole B(r) forms), WIN-013 (WGS84 matching), WIN-056 (solar elevation/23.45° tilt), κ = 1.67 nT/μGal (tautological monitoring), Schumann damping ratio (26%), and NMP drift rate (55×exp(−0.08×(year−2015))). It directly addresses the dome's strongest defense (λ_g reuse across domains) and adds the crucial 'fitted then locked ≠ derived then tested' distinction. Summary identifies at least seven fitted parameters.
change_summary: EXP-086 full rewrite addressing all c2 holes: WIN-012→WIN-053 misattribution fixed, both B(r) forms covered, 'undisclosed' reframed to 'openly published but not derived', λ_g defense preempted, κ tautology added, fitted-then-locked distinction made explicit, summary parameter count added.

holes_found.description: SUMMARY LISTS PARAMETERS NOT DISCUSSED IN BODY: The summary paragraph lists '(5) Finsler scaling function n(r)' and '(6) equatorial ring radius r_eq = 14,105 km' as fitted parameters, but neither is discussed anywhere in the preceding body paragraphs. A dome defender could challenge: 'You listed eight items but only argued for six. Where is the evidence that n(r) and r_eq are fitted?' The Finsler scaling function n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) contains the constant 0.20, and r_eq = 14,105 km appears in WIN-057 (two-zone disc topology) — both ARE fitted, but the reader has no way to verify this from the section text alone. Either add brief body paragraphs for these two, or move them to a footnote with one-line justifications.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one-sentence justifications for n(r) and r_eq in the body text, or modify the summary to explicitly note these are documented elsewhere (e.g., 'see WIN-065 and WIN-057 detail panels'). The summary's credibility depends on every listed item being either argued in the section or clearly cross-referenced.

holes_found.description: DEFENSE NOT FULLY PREEMPTED: 'Zero fitted in the test phase' interpretation. The dome's 'all ECM constants locked before live data pulled' could be interpreted more charitably as: 'We built the model (using whatever data), froze ALL constants, then tested against new data without adjusting anything. Zero parameters were fitted during testing.' This is actually how legitimate predictive models work — you train on historical data and test on new data. Our section addresses this with 'fitted then locked ≠ derived then tested,' but a sophisticated defender could still argue: 'You're holding us to a standard no model meets. Newton's G was measured from data too. The point is we didn't adjust after freezing.' We need to distinguish between (a) constants with independent theoretical derivation that happen to match data, and (b) constants reverse-engineered from the very data they claim to predict. The dome's κ = 10.9/6.5 is category (b) — the 'prediction' IS the measurement, just frozen.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a sentence after the 'fitted then locked' paragraph that distinguishes two kinds of 'locked constants': those derived from independent theory (legitimate, like Newton's G derived from multiple independent experiments) vs. those extracted from the same data they monitor (circular, like κ monitoring its own source eclipse). This closes the 'no model meets that standard' escape hatch.

holes_found.description: PARAMETER COUNT INCONSISTENCY: TLDR says 'at least seven parameters,' body summary says 'at least seven,' but the numbered list shows eight items: (1–2) B(r) coefficients, (3) κ, (4) Schumann damping, (5) n(r), (6) r_eq, (7–8) NMP constants. The '1–2' and '7–8' notation is clear but 'at least seven' understates by one. Should be 'at least eight' or the numbering should be adjusted. A pedantic defender would note this arithmetic error.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'at least seven' to 'at least eight' in both the TLDR and the summary paragraph, or renumber to (1) B(r) coefficient (since both versions have fitted amplitudes), (2) κ, (3) damping, (4) n(r), (5) r_eq, (6) NMP rate, (7) NMP decay — seven items.

holes_found.description: IGRF-13 RANGE SLIGHTLY IMPRECISE: Text states 'IGRF-13 gives ~58,000–62,000 nT at the north and ~64,000–67,000 nT at the south.' IGRF-13 (2020 epoch) gives approximately 55,000–60,000 nT near the north magnetic pole and 64,000–67,000 nT near the south. The north range is slightly overstated. The dome's 62,376 nT actually falls ABOVE the stated IGRF range, which would strengthen our argument ('the dome's coefficient exceeds IGRF values') but currently the text implies 62,376 falls within range. Not a critical issue since the approximate markers ('~') provide cover, but tightening would help.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider narrowing to '~55,000–60,000 nT at the north' to make clear that the dome's 62,376 nT exceeds even IGRF estimates, or add a note that the dome's value is above the observed range, suggesting it was tuned for best-fit rather than taken from direct measurement.

holes_found.description: WIN-013 ARGUMENT COULD BE SHARPER: The section says 'If the dome's formula reproduces WGS84, one of two things is true: either the dome independently derived the same result from different physics (no derivation is shown), or the dome's formula was fitted to WGS84 values.' This is logically correct but misses a third possibility that a defender would raise: the dome's formula reproduces WGS84 BECAUSE the physical reality is the same regardless of geometry — gravity IS what it IS regardless of whether you model Earth as a sphere or a disc. Standard physics would say this is because gravity depends on mass distribution, not shape labeling. The dome could argue 'of course our formula matches — it describes the same physical reality.' Our counter should be: reproducing an existing formula under a different label is not a prediction.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief acknowledgment: 'A defender might argue that gravity measurements are geometry-independent — but that concedes the point: if the dome's formula reproduces WGS84 regardless of underlying geometry, the dome geometry adds no predictive content. The formula works because WGS84 works, not because the dome is correct.'

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's model.txt explicitly states 'Version 50.6 — All values physically derived or observationally constrained.' The qualifier 'observationally constrained' is an admission that some values are fitted to observations — directly contradicting 'zero fitted parameters.' Our section doesn't cite this self-contradictory header. The dome's own description page distinguishes between 'physically derived' and 'observationally constrained' — meaning even the dome acknowledges two categories of constants, one of which is fitted.

The dome's live power page (line 160) says 'Constants Locked — All ECM constants (H₀, λg, κ, va) locked before live data pulled.' This helpfully enumerates four constants by name. But we identify at least eight fitted parameters — meaning the dome's own enumeration is incomplete. The gap between the dome's four named constants and our eight identified parameters could be highlighted: the dome doesn't even count all its own constants.

kernel_of_truth.description: The rewrite's kernel section is excellent — it correctly identifies that a single scale length appearing across domains WOULD be impressive if rigorously derived. The parsimony aspiration is genuine scientific reasoning, and in standard physics, natural length scales (Earth's radius, Debye length) do propagate across phenomena. The dome's instinct here reflects real physics methodology: seek unified parameters. The 'fitted then locked' approach is also closer to real science than pure curve-fitting — it at least attempts to distinguish training from testing, even if the execution fails.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The derivation chain runs backward (data → λ_g → 'prediction'), H(r) was fitted to reconcile contradictory measurements (not predicted a priori), and every domain requires additional domain-specific fitted constants that the dome doesn't count. The fact that the aspiration is correct makes the execution failure more significant, not less — the dome knows what 'zero fitted parameters' should mean but doesn't achieve it.

text_fingerprint.note: Post-EXP086 rewrite. Substantially improved from c2 version (3247→6932 chars, 6→10 paragraphs). All c2 major holes addressed.

quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Minor: κ truncation (1.6769→1.67) could be noted. IGRF ranges slightly imprecise (see holes). Otherwise all arithmetic checks out.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A sophisticated dome defender would concede that amplitude coefficients exist but reframe them: 'Every physical model has boundary conditions. The gravitational constant G is also measured from data — would you say Newtonian gravity has a fitted parameter? Our claim is that λ_g = 8,619 km is derived from firmament geometry and then appears independently in six domains. The amplitude coefficients are boundary conditions specific to each domain, just like standard physics uses different boundary conditions for different problems. And our constants were ALL locked before live monitoring began — we did not adjust after the fact. You're holding dome cosmology to a standard that no physical theory meets.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: The boundary-condition defense is now largely neutralized by the rewrite's 'fitted then locked ≠ derived then tested' argument and the κ tautology example. The remaining gap is the G-analogy: address it by noting that G has been measured independently by dozens of experiments over centuries with consistent results, while the dome's coefficients each trace to a single measurement event with no independent confirmation. The difference between 'independently replicated constant' and 'single-source constant' is the key distinction.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: None. WIN-053 misattribution from c2 is now fixed. B(r) correctly attributed to WIN-053 with both single-pole and two-pole forms. WIN-013 gravity/WGS84 argument consistent with WIN-013 detail panel. WIN-056 solar elevation/23.45° consistent with WIN-056 detail. κ discussion correctly references WIN-012. Schumann damping references are consistent with WIN-029.

deeper_analysis: This rewrite is a substantial improvement over the c2 version. Every major hole identified in the SEC-6.1-params.c2.json review has been addressed: WIN-012→WIN-053 misattribution fixed, 'undisclosed' reframed to 'openly published but not derived,' dome's λ_g defense directly confronted, κ tautology added as concrete evidence, and the 'fitted then locked ≠ derived then tested' distinction explicitly stated. The section now runs to ~6900 chars across 10 paragraphs — more than double the c2 version — with each paragraph making a specific, well-sourced argument. The remaining issues are moderate-to-minor: two summary parameters without body discussion (n(r) and r_eq), a minor count inconsistency (seven vs eight), and one unpreempted defense variant (the G-analogy). The defense_survives score drops from 3 (c2) to 1 (c3) — the boundary-condition and 'undisclosed' defenses are now effectively neutralized. The section's credibility has increased significantly. The dome author would need to genuinely derive their constants from first principles (which they cannot) to counter this argument, rather than simply pointing to labeling errors in our text.

citations_verified: IGRF-13 referenced for pole field strengths (approximate ranges)
OPEN-011 correctly cited as the dome's own unsolved problem
BOU 2017 eclipse correctly identified as κ source
citations_unchecked: IGRF-13 exact ranges for north pole field intensity should be cross-checked against official IGRF coefficients — our approximate range may be slightly overstated
